name: Sierra de Alcarama Circular Route
name_original: Sierra de Alcarama, Collado de la Mesta y Sárnago – trail running circular desde Las Fuesas
region: Tierras Altas Y Valle Del Tera
description: This difficult 9.9-mile (15.9 km) trail running route from Las Fuesas explores the rugged Sierra de Alcarama, passing Collado de la Mesta an
region_intro: Jogging around Tierras Altas Y Valle Del Tera offers a diverse landscape for runners. The region features a mix of reservoir-side paths, natural reserves, and mountain trails, providing varied terrain. Runners can explore areas characterized by dense woodlands, open valleys, and gentle hills. The elevation changes across the routes range from minimal to more challenging ascents, catering to different fitness levels. Best jogging routes around Tierras Altas Y Valle Del Tera The most popular jogging route is Embalse Cuerda del Pozo – running circular desde Vinuesa, a 10.4 miles (16.7 km) difficult trail that takes 1 hour 54 minutes to complete, offering scenic views around the reservoir. Another top favourite among local runners is Mirador del Acebal y La Turruca – trail running por la Reserva Natural del Acebal de Garagüeta, a moderate 3.4 miles (5.5 km) path that winds through a natural reserve, leading to a viewpoint. Local runners also love the Sierra de Alcarama Circular Route, a 9.9 miles (15.9 km) trail leading through the Sierra de Alcarama mountain range, often completed in about 1 hour 57 minutes. Jogging around Tierras Altas Y Valle Del Tera is defined by reservoir paths, natural reserves, and mountain trails. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy jogging routes to more challenging running trails. The routes in Tierras Altas Y Valle Del Tera are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 3.7 stars from over 5 reviews. More than 400 runners have used komoot to explore Tierras Altas Y Valle Del Tera's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many running routes are available in Tierras Altas Y Valle Del Tera?
A: There are over 45 dedicated running routes in Tierras Altas Y Valle Del Tera, offering a wide range of options for all fitness levels.

Q: Are there easy jogging routes for beginners in the region?
A: Yes, Tierras Altas Y Valle Del Tera offers several routes suitable for beginners. While most are moderate, there is at least one easy route, and many moderate trails can be enjoyed at a gentler pace. For a relatively flat and scenic option, consider the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40014075" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puente del Canto loop from El Torilejo&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What is the typical length of running trails in Tierras Altas Y Valle Del Tera?
A: Running trails in the region vary significantly in length. You can find shorter routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1064111784/mirador-del-acebal-y-la-turruca-trail-running-por-la-reserva-natural-del-acebal-de-garagueeta" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Mirador del Acebal y La Turruca&lt;/a&gt; at around 5.5 km, up to longer, more challenging runs such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/1287411" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of Vinuesa – Molinos de Duero loop from Vinuesa&lt;/a&gt; which is nearly 19 km.

Q: Are there circular running routes in Tierras Altas Y Valle Del Tera?
A: Absolutely. Many of the routes are designed as loops, allowing you to start and finish at the same point. Examples include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1064115251/castro-alto-de-la-cruz-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castro Alto de la Cruz Loop&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e990077859/thorny-broom-ra-bridge-hermitage-of-lomos-de-orios-circular" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Thorny broom, Ra Bridge, Hermitage of Lomos de Orios – circular&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Can I bring my dog on the running trails?
A: Many trails in Tierras Altas Y Valle Del Tera are dog-friendly, especially those in natural areas. However, it's always recommended to keep your dog on a leash, particularly in nature reserves or areas with livestock, and to check local regulations for specific routes. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/14939483" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Valdeavellano de Tera loop&lt;/a&gt; is a good option for a run with your canine companion.

Q: When is the best time of year to go jogging in Tierras Altas Y Valle Del Tera?
A: The region is beautiful year-round, but spring and autumn offer the most pleasant temperatures for running, with vibrant foliage or blooming wildflowers. Summer mornings and evenings are also ideal to avoid the midday heat. Some routes, like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1064104577/gr-86-san-pedro-manrique-to-vea-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;GR-86: San Pedro Manrique to Vea Trail&lt;/a&gt;, can be enjoyed across multiple seasons.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on the running routes?
A: Tierras Altas Y Valle Del Tera offers a varied landscape. You'll find routes along reservoir-side paths, through dense woodlands, across open valleys, and up gentle hills. Expect a mix of dirt trails, gravel paths, and sometimes paved sections, catering to different preferences.

Q: Are there any running routes that offer opportunities for wildlife spotting?
A: Yes, the natural reserves and forested areas are excellent for wildlife spotting. Routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1064111784/mirador-del-acebal-y-la-turruca-trail-running-por-la-reserva-natural-del-acebal-de-garagueeta" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Mirador del Acebal y La Turruca&lt;/a&gt;, which passes through the Reserva Natural del Acebal de Garagüeta, offer chances to see local fauna and flora.

Q: What do other runners enjoy most about jogging in Tierras Altas Y Valle Del Tera?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 3.7 stars from over 5 reviews. Runners often praise the diverse scenery, from tranquil reservoir views to challenging mountain trails, and the opportunity to connect with nature. The variety of routes ensures there's something for every ability.

Q: Are there any running routes suitable for families with children?
A: For families, look for shorter, less challenging routes with minimal elevation gain. While specific 'family-friendly' designations aren't always available, routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/14939440" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Running loop from Vinuesa&lt;/a&gt; offer a moderate distance and manageable terrain that can be enjoyed by active families.

Q: Is parking generally available near the trailheads?
A: In many villages and popular starting points for routes, you will find designated parking areas. For specific routes, it's advisable to check the route details on komoot, as local parking conditions can vary. Many routes, especially those starting from towns like Vinuesa or Valdeavellano de Tera, offer convenient access.
tips: We are walking through the Sierra de Alcarama, located in the Collado de la Mesta and with an excellent panoramic view of the nature that surrounds us.
Beautiful and well-kept refuge that we find in the Collado de la Mesta, with excellent views of the steep climb to the top of the Sierra de Alcarama.
Nice views of the town of Sárnago while we enjoy hiking on a wide and quiet dirt road.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this trail running route?
faqs.answer: The terrain on this circular route is generally rugged and varied, typical of the Sierra de Alcarama. You can expect a mix of surfaces, including eroded slopes, some rocky sections, and forest tracks. The path often enters and exits ravines and valleys, requiring good footing and attention.

faqs.question: Is this trail suitable for beginners in trail running?
faqs.answer: Given its 'difficult' rating, rugged terrain, and nearly 475 meters of elevation gain over 15.9 km, this trail is not recommended for beginners. It requires a good level of fitness and experience with varied, challenging terrain.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along the route?
faqs.answer: This trail offers several notable features. You'll pass through the scenic Collado de la Mesta, Sierra de Alcarama, and near the Collado de la Mesta Refuge. A highlight is also the View of Sárnago, offering a glimpse of the abandoned village and the surrounding dry, mountainous landscape.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the trailhead in Las Fuesas?
faqs.answer: The trail starts and ends in Las Fuesas. While specific parking details for this exact trailhead are not provided, Las Fuesas is a small village, and you should be able to find suitable roadside parking or a small designated area within the village to begin your run.

faqs.question: Is this trail dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: Information regarding specific dog regulations for this exact trail is not available. However, when trail running with dogs in natural areas like the Sierra de Alcarama, it's generally advisable to keep them on a leash to protect local wildlife and ensure their safety on rugged terrain. Always carry water for your dog.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to run this trail?
faqs.answer: The Sierra de Alcarama region can experience hot summers and cold winters. The best times for trail running are typically spring (April-May) and autumn (September-October) when temperatures are milder and the landscape offers pleasant conditions. Avoid the peak summer heat and potential winter snow or ice, which can make the rugged terrain more challenging.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or entrance fees required for this trail or the Sierra de Alcarama y Valle del Alhama park?
faqs.answer: Generally, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required for trail running on routes like this within the Sierra de Alcarama y Valle del Alhama region. However, always respect local regulations, especially concerning environmental protection and private property. There are no indications of fees for accessing this particular route.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this trail running route?
faqs.answer: This trail is approximately 15.9 km long with significant elevation gain. For an experienced trail runner, it typically takes around 1 hour and 57 minutes to complete. However, your actual time may vary depending on your fitness level, pace, and how often you stop to enjoy the views.

faqs.question: What kind of scenery can I expect along the way?
faqs.answer: The route offers a dry, mountainous landscape characterized by reforested pine areas, matorral (shrubland), and open views of surrounding hills and dales. The presence of the abandoned village of Sárnago adds a unique historical and cultural dimension to the natural exploration.

faqs.question: Are there any facilities like cafes or pubs near the trailhead in Las Fuesas or in Sárnago?
faqs.answer: Las Fuesas is a small village, and Sárnago is an abandoned village, so facilities like cafes or pubs directly at the trailhead or along the route are unlikely. It's highly recommended to carry all necessary supplies, including water and snacks, for the duration of your run. You might find amenities in larger nearby towns.

faqs.question: Is this a circular route or an out-and-back trail?
faqs.answer: This is a circular trail running route, meaning it starts and ends in the same location, Las Fuesas, without requiring you to retrace your steps.

waypoints.name: Collado de la Mesta, Sierra de Alcarama
